Consider 4e023039f415b01cf1b0d4b493d3fc95649d045d, which I think serves the use-case you presented. By exposing the value as a class attribute, it also enables other ways to override encoding:

```
class MyConnection(ServerConnection):
    transmit_encoding = 'latin-1'
```

or 

```
class MyComplexEncodingConnection(ServerConnection):
    def encode(self, msg):
        return msg.encode('utf-8') if random.randint(1,2) == 1 else msg.encode('latin-1')
```

But of course, you can still simply set the transmit_encoding attribute on an instance of the class and all messages transmitted subsequently will use that encoding:

```
conn = ServerConnection()
conn.transmit_encoding = 'latin-1'
```

Let me know if that works for you and I'll cut a release, or if it doesn't how it could be improved.

@DanEEStar the way I handle time at least in synchronous tests is that I use a datetime-mock library, my preferred one is [freezegun](https://github.com/spulec/freezegun) but feel free to use any other. The trick is that there's usually a function to advance the frozen clock manually, for `freezegun` it is
```
frozen_datetime.tick(delta=datetime.timedelta(seconds=10))
```
With that in mind I mock `time.sleep` function not to wait for wallclock time to elapse but rather to advance the frozen clock. This kills two birds with one stone: sleep returns right away and the tests don't get stuck for a long time, but you also get the time invariant back, that is after you invoke `time.sleep`  `datetime.now()` returns a timestamp in the future. It could be slightly more involved for asynchronous tasks where the time can affect some sort of an event loop, but it's not impossible.

Minimap2 generally does local alignment. A gap towards the end will lead to a lower alignment score, so it is clipped. You can use
```
minimap2 -c -x asm5 --end-bonus=20
```
to ask minimap2 to prefer end-to-end alignment.

The main motivation is some code modernisation and following the Django-recommended ways:

- Moving the status field inside the Message model: [Django Docs: choices](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/models/fields/#choices)
- Moving the signal to apps.py/signals.py: [Django Docs: Signals - Where should this code live](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/signals/#connecting-receiver-functions)
- Running tests: [Django docs: Running tests](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/testing/overview/#running-tests)

Regarding the tests, you could make a point for having a seperate tests directory such as described [here](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/testing/advanced/#using-the-django-test-runner-to-test-reusable-applications). However, this is rather obscure compared to just 'python manage.py test' which every Django person/project knows and uses, and the latter also integrates better with coverage and/or other packages.

- Formatting + sorting imports should be done using flake8/pep8. Regarding the encoding line, this was not intentional and is probably the result of other edits.

@cannatag I believe you should first make sure that the [optional] `requestValue` field is present in the response by calling `.isValue` (or [.hasValue()](http://pyasn1.sourceforge.net/docs/type/univ/octetstring.html#pyasn1.type.univ.OctetString.hasValue)), if it evaluates to `True` you can attempt to evaluate the value e.g. not the other way round:

    if request['requestValue'].isValue and request['requestValue']:
        pass

Here is a small screencast on the matter:

    >>> from pyasn1_modules import rfc2251
    >>> r = rfc2251.ExtendedRequest()
    >>> list(r)
    ['requestName', 'requestValue']
    >>> r['requestName'].isValue
    False
    >>> r['requestValue'].isValue
    False
    >>> r['requestName'] = 'xxx'
    >>> r['requestName'].isValue
    True
    >>> if r['requestValue']: pass
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
    pyasn1.error.PyAsn1Error: Uninitialized ASN.1 value ("__nonzero__" attribute looked up)
    >>> r['requestValue'] = 'yyy'
    >>> r['requestValue'].isValue

    >>> if r['requestValue']: pass

Perhaps `cell_data` is too much generic for enconding regions? For me, `point_data` and `cell_data` means scalar or vector fields on either points or cells, in the VTK sense. The gmsh format also support point and cell data (real values, not integers!), but confusing/mixing such data with regions was the root cause of my initial complaints.

If you still want to encode regions with cell data, that's fine, but we need to devise a mechanism to distinguish integral values (for gmsh regions, aka physical/elementary tags) from scalar/vector data (for gmsh `NodeData` or `ElementData`).

This is great. The latitude change is definitely correct. I think the longitude change is a bit more complex because latitudes wrap around at +/- 180degrees. For instance, if you have a layer of points in Alaska and a layer of points in Kamchatka, you would expect the map to show points centered around the international date line, but, as it stands, it would show the entire world: ```python LAT = 65.0 pts_west = [[LAT, 170.0], [LAT, 175.0]] pts_east = [[LAT, -170.0], [LAT, -175.0]] m = gmaps.Map() m.add_layer(gmaps.symbol_layer(pts_west, fill_color="red", stroke_color="red")) m.add_layer(gmaps.symbol_layer(pts_east, fill_color="green", stroke_color="green")) m ``` This is admittedly an edge case that only applies for layers centered around the international date line, but we might as well get it right. An easy solution to this is to re-use the `bounds.longitude_bounds` which calculates approximate bounds by fitting a von Mises distribution to the underlying data. For instance, we could try something like (untested): ``` max_longitudes = [bounds[1][1] for bounds in bounds_list] max_longitude = bounds.longitude_bounds(max_longitudes)[1] ``` Any thoughts?

Okay...that makes sense as far as why it so slow.

It probably would only take about 20 seconds in total (if concurrency was used and only one SSH connection per device).

You can see if can get it to go faster by setting `global_delay_factor` which is an argument to ConnectHandler to .5 or to .2.

You would also probably need to modify the delay_factor argument into send_config_set() and set it to the same value as you set ConnectHandler to.

I can point you where these modifications need made in the Netmiko source code if you want to experiment with them.

To me this is really fundamentally an openstack issue (most notably you must have concurrency when dealing with SSH connections).

That being said you might be able to cut the Netmiko total time significantly by being more aggressive in reducing sleep times that are embedded into Netmiko.

To figure out the exact raster, you need to do a bit of juggling. You need to use `mdf.getChannelData(mdf.getChannelMaster(channelName))` to get the master time vector for a given channel, then do a simple division of the second and first values, round that off to the nearest hundredth, and that should give you the measure rate. But i don't think that's what you want to do.

If you must sort the channels by measure rate, you can simply use the name of the master channel as rasters. Alternatively, you could resample the file to a known measure rate, and everything will be aligned. I think this latter option is probably what you need.

If your measure files are large, you can load only certain channels (assuming you don't need all of them). I think the option is called `channelList`, and you can use it when loading the file. You can also use `noDataLoading=True` so only the channel names are loaded.

@csware: `SetHandler none` has no effect (specifically, it tells Apache to ignore a previously forced handler; you're probably searching for `SetHandler default-handler`). Instead of `mod_headers`, one should use the `ForceType` core directive, what incidentally also bypasses all handlers (i.e. `SetHandler` isn't necessary at all).

Additionally, directory listing should be disabled explicitly and no other contents should be served. A `RedirectMatch 404` with a negative lookahead regex handles this job quiet well and also prevents information disclosure (i.e. requesting `.well-known/acme-challenge` returns `404 Not Found` instead of `403 Forbidden`).

```apache
Alias /.well-known/acme-challenge/ /var/www/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/
<Directory "/var/www/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/">
    Options None
    AllowOverride None
    ForceType text/plain
    RedirectMatch 404 "^(?!/\.well-known/acme-challenge/[\w-]{43}$)"
</Directory>
```

Dear Ricardo,

This library does not depend on http/https protocol, it is purely transport implementation. Let me address your questions in details.

1. If the use-case is to connect to json-rpc server, than there is no point to use any "json-rpc-like" library. Python requests would do the job. Even though this library supports client connection, I personally think it is overhead to use "json-rpc" for client side.
If use-case is to implement json-rpc server, this library would be helpful.

2. In terms of protocol usage, "json-rpc" does not limit developers to any of them. One may possible to use it with http/https (Django/Tornado etc.) as well as message queue servers (RabbitMQ+pika). As a benefit, it does not require developer to choose specific framework.

As I understood from your question, Django works as a web server on tcp port, https connections are proxied via Apache. In this case https implementation is purely on Apache side, while Django does not know anything about it (well, except settings, such as secure cookie handler).

This library would handle this case, in fact optional Django connector would simplify views development. Please refer to the documentation for more information: https://json-rpc.readthedocs.org/en/latest/django_integration.html

I don't have any specific ideas. I think it's just a matter of figuring out how to manage internal state ("what I know about the world at some particular time") with external state ("what I _could_ know about the world at some particular time"). And in particular, making sure you don't expend gratuitous resources in managing that state. For example, the player updating script goes through numerous heuristics to avoid sending a lot of additional HTTP requests to NFL.com. The initial crawl will send a lot, but future updates are incremental.

As a clear example, "updating the schedule" should not involve "sending dozens of HTTP requests to download the entire schedule for the past several years." That might seem obvious, but it's just an example. :)

So after some investigation, I found that there wasn't a problem if you triggered the sort reset first:

```js
$('table').trigger('sortReset').trigger('filterReset');
```

But reversing the order causes the sortReset trigger to skip reapplying the widgets because it was still processing the filterReset widget reapplication.

I added a `setTimeout`, I know it's not ideal, to allow time for processing.

I think it might be a better idea to add a "resetSortAndFilter" method that combines the two methods so that you won't need to trigger multiple events.

jaydebeapi uses jpype to access java libraries. You have to import jpype and call `jpype.attachThreadToJVM()` inside your thread. Ideally you call it after jaydebeapi `connect()` has been executed. That guarantees you jpype is set up correctly.

        try:

Found the problem. The correct usage for cprofile output files is 
```$ cprofilev -f /path/to/cprofile/output```

Hi, I have actually "deprecated" DataResampler and DataReplayer in favor of the "filter" technology and the resampler and replayer filters.

The "filters" are described here: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-11-21-data-filters/data-filling-filtering/

The resampling post was updated: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-09-25-tickdata-resample/resample-tickdata/

Ways to resample/replay data:

  - Over Cerebro

    ```python
    cerebro.resampledata(data, **kwargs)  # kwargs -> same as the existing ones for DataResampler
    cerebro.replaydata(data. **kwargs)  #  # kwargs -> same as the existing ones for DataReplayer
    ```

  - Over the data itself (this is what ``cerebro`` internally does)

    ```python
    data.resample(**kwargs)
    cerebro.add(data, name=xx)  # name=None is the default
    ```

  - If you for example resample a daily data to weekly and want to use both simultaneously

    ```python
    cerebro.adddata(data)
    cerebro.resampledata(data.clone(), **kwargs)
    ```

@spradeepv ,

> I know we can capture logs, but can we redirect logs to a specific
   file format (like .htm/.xhtml)

No, this would be the same thing as the xUnit output (or an extension of it).

> When can we expect this to be completed?

This is a deceptively large/complex feature, and it is tentatively planned for the next release (v1.8.0). It might hit the master branch sooner, in a rough state. You can see the current list of fixes & enhancements planned for this release [in the milestone](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/milestones/1.8.0%20Parsing+Scoping%20Enhancements). 

Currently, xUnit output and improved variable (https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/101) use are tied for most-requested features.   Both are very large features and require rewriting some of the framework internals, so it's likely that only one will make it into the release unless I get a lot of community help on smaller items.  xUnit has actually been bumped up to finish sooner, because I found a way to implement it without requiring as much effort.

> I am willing to contribute too, if you need help.

Absolutely!  The best way to help with this to help free up time I'm spending answering questions/issues, writing documentation, and implementing small features so that I can focus on the bigger tasks.  In particular, [any issue marked "help wanted'](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/labels/help%20wanted) is something I'd welcome a community PR for.  In particular, https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/100 really needs some work and would be easy for somebody to take on; this would reduce the number of questions tremendously if done right.

The xUnit feature itself depends on a moderately complex refactoring of the test execution logic (moving it into the test/benchmark classes and using callbacks to do logging).  See https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/163 item A1 for details of that.  As it stands, I expect some issues with the inter-module dependencies (and testing), but just finished the 1.7.x release last weekend and haven't had time to dig further yet.

> Is there a way to disable a test? If not, can we introduce one?

Well, you can always comment out the appropriate sections of the YAML.  It's something to consider down the road, but would probably have to be part of the 1.9.0 or [2.0.0 milestone](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/milestones/2.0%20-%20Major%20internal%20refactoring%20for%20extensibility)  because to do it in a useful way (reporting skipped tests, not just pass/fail) would make more sense once the logging and config parsing allow it (1.8/1.9).

By the way, if you'd like to discuss overall project plans, I'd like to ask that this be done in either the Gitter channel (link on the main README) or in the issues for the features in question (it's hard to track discussions in closed issues). 

Regards,
Sam

I've been thinking more along the lines of what bitcoind does:

- on startup, you have a hard coded list of servers.  You use this if you have no recent peer history, otherwise you use your own peer history (perhaps mixing in the hard coded servers)
- stored peer history includes things like what is currently on IRC, but also time of last attempted connection, time of last good connection, whether server successfully answered a challenge (e.g. a random UTXO)
- you connect to, and maintain, 8 outgoing peer connections, taken semi-randomly from the DB, but attempting to avoid easy sybil attacks.
- do a peers.subscribe to those 8 servers like the electrum client does, but pass extra args as your own settings, i.e. what is currently on IRC but perhaps also to include ToR.  In ElectrumX I will change peers.subscribe to be a subscription, and it will send notifications of new peers.  If this optional extra argument is passed, it will assume the incoming connection is a server and add it to its peers list.  - add unknown peers to the recent history list, indicating no connection attempts etc. made
- in the background, very occasionally, choose a peer to connect to from your list, and send them a challenge if the connection is successful.  Then disconnect.  Record the results.
- replies to peers.subscribe and new peer notifications: for Electrum clients, should only send for peers that have successfully responded to a challenge (unioned with the hard-coded list?). For peer servers, perhaps we can send immediately, or should we attempt to make a connection?  Perhaps send max 30 known good, max 10 stale or untried ones, randomly selected.
- aged peers in the local history are removed after a certain amount of time (e.g. 30 days) has passed with no successful connection made.

This corresponds roughly to addr (notifications) and getaddr (peers.subscribe).  Challenges can include a random UTXO, header hash request, balance request, etc.

The idea of the challenge is to limit the ability to fill the peer lists with non-servers.

There are a few details I've missed but this is basically it.  It doesn't require any new network messages.

A server should perhaps, occasionally, connect to IRC briefly to get stragglers still using IRC (e-s and jelectrum servers?).  But we should do this less and less, and hopefully someone can implement something similar in e-s and jelectrum too.

Maybe the class implementing this should have a common core that is used by ElectrumX and Electrum client, with a view to the client moving towards becoming a little more independent and less dependent on its main connection.

If you run complex setups you need to be able to manage them yourself.  The logic is simple - to verify a peer, e-x checks that it can connect to it at its advertised address or host name, that it has basic functionality, and that the IP address the IP address of the remote peer requesting peering.

If it doesn't match then it is not valid.  If your proxying layers break this then there is not much I can do about it; the check is necessary to avoid advertising fake peers to clients.

I encountered this when using `ObjectProxy` to wrap the result of [`aiohttp.request`](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/client_reference.html#aiohttp.request).  It returns an object that can be used as an async context manager, and also a coroutine.  I want to change it's behaviour a tiny bit, and I thought an ObjectProxy would be a good way to do that.

I have worked around this issue by defining `__aenter__` & `__aexit__` methods manually on my object proxy, similar to your code sample above.

I _think_ (though I'm not an expert) defining functions that just return the result of the async methods on the wrapped object is fine - async functions and normal functions are called the same way, it's just that async functions return an awaitable, and their actual code doesn't run until that is awaited on.

`__aiter__` and `__anext__` are similar, though it does appear that python specifically checks for `__aiter__` methods when used in an async for:

```
In [1]: class Test():
   ...:     pass
   ...:

In [2]: async def test():
   ...:     async for a in Test():
   ...:         pass

In [8]: asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(test())

TypeError: 'async for' requires an object with __aiter__ method, got Test
```

So I guess providing these on the wrapper when they didn't exist on the actual object itself could change the behaviour slightly...

I think `__await__` is a different case - it's not actually an async function, but a normal function that returns an iterator.  Again, it does seem like python does some special checks on objects that are used in `await` expressions.

```
In [9]: class Test:
   ...:     def __await__(self):
   ...:         pass
   ...:

In [10]: async def test():
   ....:     await Test()
   ....:

In [11]: asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(test())

TypeError: __await__() returned non-iterator of type 'NoneType'

In [14]: class Test():
   ....:     pass
   ....:

In [15]: asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(test())

TypeError: object Test can't be used in 'await' expression
```

        text = f.read().decode('ascii', 'replace')

    with open(path, "w") as f:
        print(text, file=f)

It depends. 

- [NFS should not work with inotify](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4231243/inotify-with-nfs)
- Samba (CIFS) does not work with inotify (my own experience)
- SSHFS does not work with inotify (my own experience)

So, to conclude, you're out of luck unless you want to write the inotify broker mentioned in the SO answer.

A side note: At PyPi index, there is a section with Caveats. I haven't found anything about network drives there, though it should be mentioned there in my view.

Hi @RobRoseKnows. Take a look at the following blog post for a detailed description on using the address API with flask-ask: https://data-dive.com/alexa-get-device-location-from-custom-skill

Basically, you need to get the deviceId and consentToken from the user request. This can be done in flask-ask by retrieving the following elements:
context.System.device.deviceId
context.System.user.permissions.consentToken

In addition, the PR @johnwheeler  suggested can be used if the request does not provide a consentToken. This means, the user has not given your skill permission to access his address. Hence, you can ask for it with the consent_card. 

Neither the context object nor the consent_card is documented yet. I might give it a shot, if I find some time.

The problem is with ``libCryptoki2_64.so`` (not PyKCS11 😃).

PyKCS11 calls ``C_GetInfo()`` to know if the library is initialized or not. PyKCS11 is expecting ``CKR_CRYPTOKI_NOT_INITIALIZED`` and would then call ``C_Initialize()`` but ``libCryptoki2_64.so`` returns ``CKR_OK`` instead.
https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/PyKCS11/blob/master/src/pkcs11lib.cpp#L79

Then ``libCryptoki2_64.so`` continues to return ``CKR_OK`` to ``C_GetSlotList()`` even if the library is still _not_ initialized.

One way to fix the problem would be to always call ``C_Initialize()`` in ``Load()`` (and remove the use of ``m_bAutoInitialized``)
https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/PyKCS11/blob/master/src/pkcs11lib.cpp#L54

I have seen this, it's much cleaner and more lightweight than jupyterthemes. The primary difference is that jupyterthemes uses Less to define all the user and theme settings as variables which then get compiled into the custom.css file using lesscpy. That way new themes are a little easier to develop (assuming you just want to alter the color scheme and not major changes to the layout, notebook formatting, etc..). You would just add a new .less file to jupyterthemes/styles/ directory, copy and paste all the variables from one of the other themes, and change the hexcodes to your liking. 

So, depending on who you ask, that's one benefit of jupyterthemes. But by the same token it's quite a bit messier, less straight forward than jupyter-themer... by a wide margin. Also, jupyterthemes includes code for customizing the layout of some pretty narrowly used features (i.e., nb-extensions page, command palette, etc.) so if you don't use those, then it's pretty obnoxious working around that code if you're trying to modify something in the source code. 

Finally, jupyter-themer has a ton more themes to choose from. I think going forward, it would be nice to have all (or most) of those syntax highlight themes available as jupyterthemes styles.

`_PROXY_DJANGO_SETTINGS` should be set to true when django inits the app: https://github.com/markfinger/python-react/blob/78deb9479a302ce226dd5b24c0aaeabe0ebd79ad/react/apps.py#L10

If you're using an older version of django that doesn't support app config, you may want to run the following at some point.

```python
react.conf.settings._PROXY_DJANGO_SETTINGS = True
react.conf.settings.configure(
    **getattr(settings, 'REACT', {})
)
```

If you need to do that, make sure you don't import react.conf until your settings module has completed, somewhere in your views or urls would work. 

There are layers that provide optional django support and they try to import the settings module as a test to detect if django is active. If you import react.conf.settings, you'll trigger a circular import and those layers will silently fail and assume django isn't running.

Hi friend (noticed the star) -- venv-update (pip-faster) instruments (monkeypatches) pip to hook into the dependency resolution and has two goals:
- speed up cached wheels that are `==`d in requirements files
- prune installed packages that are no longer depended on (`--prune`)

The venv-update plugin of `tox-pip-extensions` simply adds another stage which calls `pip-faster install ... --prune` with the total set of dependencies after tox has handled installation.  There's some slight overhead of rerunning setup.py for each invocation, but having up-to-date dependencies is _way_ worth it.

What this article refers to is something different than the XML-RPC API. So the basic functionality is not related to this.
What we do with the XML-API (addon) is fetching the device names from the CCU. That's only optional though, and I actually don't think anybody uses the XML-API for that. If you _can_ use the addon, then you have a CCU. And with a CCU you always can use the JSON-RPC API, which is supported as well.
The API-URL in the link you have posted indeed is different than the one we have implemented. It was like it is in the current code once. If they have changed it it _could_ be changed in pyhomematic as well. But then we would need to support two possible endpoints, and that only for resolving names, which can be done via JSON-RPC already. So if they really broke the API-URL I want to just leave it as it is. It's not worth the effort for a _useless_ (duplicate) function.

Django passes an UUID value to a backend as a Python UUID object if the backend supports UUID natively, otherwise as a 32-character hexadecimal string without hyphens. Unfortunately no value transformation hook on `UUIDField` is avaliable for backends unlike other standard model fields.
https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.5/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L2375-L2377

As you can see in the pyodbc test code, pyodbc only accepts a string value for an `uniqueidentifier` column. And to make things worse, SQL Server only accepts a 36-character string with hyphens as an `uniqueidentifer`. It would be easy to implement this feature if one of the following conditions is met in the future, otherwise it would be tricky.

- pyodbc supports UUID natively (acceepts Python UUID object as an `uniqueidentifier` value)
- SQL Server accepts 32-character hexadecimal string without hyphens as an `uniqueidentifier`
- Django provides a value transformation hook on `UUIDField` for backend adapters to add hyphens to 32-character UUID string

You can disconnect a client as a response to a Socket.IO event, so this would be in the Socket.IO server process itself. Just call `socketio.disconnect(sid)`. The client can send a `close` event, and then in the handler for this event you can do the disconnect. Another option is to call `socket.disconnect()` on the client. And finally, note that browser clients are automatically disconnected when they navigate away to another page.

Hello @gzh1991, thanks for aaking this question, as I'm sure it's of interest to a few users. 

I've recently had similar results,  and can share what my measurements have shown.  To find out exactly why it's slower on your machine I recommend using nvprof to look at the perf summaries for both fp32 and fp16.

What I have noted is that fp16 requires kernel launches with typically more registers used per thread than fp32.  There's a fixed number of registers per processor in your gpu, so cuda responds to this increase by decreasing the number of threads running concurrently.  This obviously lowers perf.  

So in the general case fp16 might run back little slower, but the big caveat is if you're using hardware that has special instructions to accelerate fp16 matrix mults.  Currently this means you'd need to have a V100 (as in the aws p3 instance) or a Titan V.  If you have this hardware you should see a quite dramatic increase in performance for network  layers that are supported (for example fully connected layers).  If you don't have one of those gpus you're probably better off sticking with fp32 fir training unless you're memory constrained

I have this code: https://github.com/edmBernard/mxnet_example_shared_weight/blob/master/demo_with_gluon.py
```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd, autograd
from mxnet import gluon
import numpy as np

ctx = mx.cpu()

batch_size = 128
num_inputs = 784
num_outputs = 10

# Get MNIST Data
def transform(data, label):
    return data.astype(np.float32)/255, label.astype(np.float32)
train_data1 = mx.gluon.data.DataLoader(mx.gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=True, transform=transform), batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_data1 = mx.gluon.data.DataLoader(mx.gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=False, transform=transform), batch_size, shuffle=False)
train_data2 = mx.gluon.data.DataLoader(mx.gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=True, transform=transform), batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_data2 = mx.gluon.data.DataLoader(mx.gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=False, transform=transform), batch_size, shuffle=False)

net_shared = gluon.nn.Sequential()
with net_shared.name_scope():
    net_shared.add(gluon.nn.Dense(128, activation='relu'))
    net_shared.add(gluon.nn.Dense(64, activation='relu'))

net_mod1 = gluon.nn.Sequential()
with net_mod1.name_scope():
    net_mod1.add(gluon.nn.Dense(num_outputs, activation='relu'))

net_mod2 = gluon.nn.Sequential()
with net_mod2.name_scope():
    net_mod2.add(gluon.nn.Dense(num_outputs, activation='relu'))

net_shared.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Uniform(scale=0.1), ctx=ctx)
net_mod1.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Uniform(scale=0.1), ctx=ctx)
net_mod2.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Uniform(scale=0.1), ctx=ctx)

softmax_cross_entropy = gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss()

trainer_shared = gluon.Trainer(net_shared.collect_params(), 'sgd', {'learning_rate': 0.05})
trainer_mod1 = gluon.Trainer(net_mod1.collect_params(), 'sgd', {'learning_rate': 0.05})
trainer_mod2 = gluon.Trainer(net_mod2.collect_params(), 'sgd', {'learning_rate': 0.05})

def evaluate_accuracy(data_iterator, net):
    acc = mx.metric.Accuracy()
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(data_iterator):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx).reshape((-1,784))
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        output = net(data)
        predictions = nd.argmax(output, axis=1)
        acc.update(preds=predictions, labels=label)
    return acc.get()[1]

epochs = 4
moving_loss = 0.
smoothing_constant = .01

print("#### Before Training ####")
test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data1, lambda x: net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data1, lambda x :net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
print("Mod1: Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (train_accuracy, test_accuracy))
test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data2, lambda x: net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data2, lambda x :net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
print("Mod2: Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (train_accuracy, test_accuracy))

print("\n#### Shared+Module1 Training ####")
for e in range(epochs):
    # Train Branch with mod1 on dataset 1 
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data1):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx).reshape((-1, 784))
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        with autograd.record():
            output = net_mod1(net_shared(data))
            loss = softmax_cross_entropy(output, label)
        loss.backward()
        trainer_shared.step(batch_size)
        trainer_mod1.step(batch_size)

        curr_loss = nd.mean(loss).asscalar()
        moving_loss = (curr_loss if ((i == 0) and (e == 0))
                       else (1 - smoothing_constant) * moving_loss + (smoothing_constant) * curr_loss)

    test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data1, lambda x: net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
    train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data1, lambda x :net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
    print("Mod1: Epoch %s. Loss: %s, Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (e, moving_loss, train_accuracy, test_accuracy))

# We expect the shared module to start where the first module finished
# There will be a small accuracy decrease since one layer was not trained
test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data2, lambda x: net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data2, lambda x :net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
print("\n#### Shared+Module2 Result after Mod1 Training ####")
print("Mod2: Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (train_accuracy, test_accuracy))
print("\n#### Shared+Module2 Training ####")
for e in range(epochs):
    # Train Branch with mod2 on dataset 2 
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data2):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx).reshape((-1,784))
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        with autograd.record():
            output = net_mod2(net_shared(data))
            loss = softmax_cross_entropy(output, label)
        loss.backward()
        trainer_shared.step(batch_size)
        trainer_mod2.step(batch_size)

        curr_loss = nd.mean(loss).asscalar()
        moving_loss = (curr_loss if ((i == 0) and (e == 0))
                       else (1 - smoothing_constant) * moving_loss + (smoothing_constant) * curr_loss)

    test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data2, lambda x: net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
    train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data2, lambda x: net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
    print("Mod2: Epoch %s. Loss: %s, Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (e, moving_loss, train_accuracy, test_accuracy))

print("\n#### After Training ####")
test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data1, lambda x: net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data1, lambda x :net_mod1(net_shared(x)))
print("Mod1: Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (train_accuracy, test_accuracy))
test_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(test_data2, lambda x: net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
train_accuracy = evaluate_accuracy(train_data2, lambda x :net_mod2(net_shared(x)))
print("Mod2: Train_acc %s, Test_acc %s" % (train_accuracy, test_accuracy))
```

@macisamuele For this functionality, one of the things I'd need to do is to add msgpack unmarshalling in bravado_core. However, to be able to do that I need to have access to the bytes of a response, which is currently not possible under the interface specified in `bravado_core.response.IncomingResponse`. So to implement this, I'd need to change IncomingResponse to have 'bytes' in the _required_attrs_, which would be a breaking change as now all implementations of this, including the `RequestsResponseAdapter` and `FidoResponseAdapter` in bravado will need to be updated to use the latest version of bravado_core if pushed. This means that this feature would require a breaking change to bravado_core. Would you say this is a feature worth having a breaking change?

I use task namespaces extensively in my projects as a means of task organization, and this issue made me question my assumptions about them (but I think it turns out they were on point).
 
@javrasya If I'm understanding you correctly, it sounds like task namespaces are really what you want to be using instead.  When a  `task_namespace` is given a value, its incorporated into `task_family` value (prefixed to `task_name`), .e.g.

```
class Foo(luigi.Task):
    task_namespace = 'custom_a'
```

Foo's `task_family` is now: `custom_a.Foo`, and as far as the scheduler is concerned, is a different from any `Foo` task class that may defined in other modules or scopes and task namespaces.  It groups them like you'd expect in the scheduler view.

If I recall though, the scheduler *used* to group the tasks up by task class name only, and didn't look at the namespace, in the sidebar view.  At some point this changed.  If at some point you tried this same technique with namespaces and it didn't work like you were hoping, try updating luigi (scheduler and workers) and see if that helps.

(see https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task_register.py#L121)

@kyleknap I believe there are a few more items to consider here

1. Multithreading doesn't necessarily imply long lived threads. Use of multithreading for parallel download of files for S3 using eventlet for example is common in Python, and for that use case it actually makes sense to create the session and resource once and reuse it from the short lived threads.

2. In addition to 1 above, but not only for that use case, there are many resources that are initialised and could be reused. I.e. the urllib3 error that is thrown often when trying to parallelise S3 reads with boto3 - "Connection pool is full, discarding connection" which points to the fact that multi-threaded usage of a shared session could be beneficial for reasons of sharing internal connection pools (see http://stackoverflow.com/a/18845952/135701, https://github.com/openstack/python-swiftclient/commit/19d7e1812a99d73785146667ae2f3a7156f06898 for example of possible solutions).

Therefore, recommending to create more instances on all multi-threaded use cases isn't a good advice. It's probably better to make Session and Resource immutable and threadsafe, and let them manage connection pool sizing etc and by that better support all multithreaded scenarios - including short lived threads used for parallelisation of I/O, and get a good performance gain while at it.

Just want to capture some of the offline discussion here:

So when we were pulling in the waiters, we realized that the implementation does not handle multiple autoscaling groups. It might be a while till we can pull this into botocore for two reasons:

1) We could try to rename the waiter by prefixing the names with "Single" to indicate that you cannot wait for multiple waiters. However, the ruby sdk already shipped this waiter and a resource model that uses the waiter and we like to make sure that we match all of the different models that we ship across SDKs for consistency sake. So if we branch off, it would be difficult to pick up updates from other SDKs to the waiter and the resource model, which is not ideal.
2) We need to update our waiter specification to take into account inputs. The only way to support waiting for multiple groups is to make sure that the length returned from DescribeAutoscalingGroups is equal to the number of group ids provided to the waiter.

If you really need this waiter, you can pull down the waiter and add it to ``~/.aws/models/autoscaling/2011-01-01/waiters-2.json`` to use it in the CLI and boto3, but I do not think at this time we can merge it into botocore until we figure out the story of handling multiple autoscaling groups.

I think I see what the issue is here. You have `sortable_field_name = 'Version'` in `AssessmentVersionInLine`. The purpose of `sortable_field_name` is to allow for drag-and-drop resorting in the admin. It should point to an `IntegerField` or `PositiveSmallIntegerField` on the model so that it can keep track of the order of the inlines (best practice is to make it a hidden field in the admin). So, upon save (and re-ordering) it will reset all of the `sortable_field_name` fields in the inlines to be their zero-indexed position within the formset.

If you want to allow users to drag-and-drop re-order the inlines, then you should create a separate field, something like:

```python
position = fields.PositiveSmallIntegerField(default=0)
```

Or, if you don't want drag-and-drop sorting, don't include the `sortable_field_name` property on your `InlineAdmin`.

(Side note: `sortable_field_name` doesn't have an effect on `NestedModelAdmin`, only on inlines)

Hello,

I have been struggling around with this issue for some hours as well. And I thought it might help somebody to describe what I found out:

As far as I can see the problem occurrs when you delete an object that has a relationship with another object that is audited as well.

Example: You have a user and the user has a profile and audit logs for both models.

- You want to delete the user "robert"
- Django first deletes the related objects - the Profile of Rboert in this case (plus others)
- if the Profile is deleted, django-audit-log is triggered and creates a ProfileAuditLogEntry ("james deleted user robert").
- Django deletes the user which now fails, because the ProfileAuditLogEntry has an FK to it! The new ProfileAuditLogEntry is a new related object that Django of course could not find when it deleted the related objects to "release" all FKs - because it was not yet there.

My workaround / solution was to use audit_log.disable_tracking() in a pre_delete signal handler of Profile. Like this, the deletion of the related object is simply not logged and the deletion of the user works fine. Of course, it is not the most elegant solution - especially if you want to track deletiion of profiles in other circumstances - but it works for us....

I hope this is helpful for somebody :)

Jörg

Previously we approached this by forcing the resolver to use a fake `sys.version_info` tuple, but that caused some errors downstream, particularly because of areas where pip makes assumptions about strings depending upon the python version and it derives that information from `sys,version_info`.  My first attempt to work around this involved overriding the `__getitem__` method of our namedtuple to allow for exceptions, which is kind of ugly: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/pull/1082/files 

However, pip only makes this assumption in one place, and since we are vendoring a patched version of pip, I believe we can just replace the conditional with some exception handling anyway.

> Assume all environment markers are "true" when generating the lock file (so everything gets listed and pinned, even items that aren't needed on the platforms generating the lock file).

Unfortunately this is the one I'm not so sure about.  All of the resolver tools in pip depend on ABI flags, so essentially we would have to tell the resolver to resolve against all possible ABI flag combinations, and not even just for the current platform.  How far does this go? Do we include different pythons as well? I haven't tested this quite yet, but I have a general sense that this will not be straightforward because of:

> (e.g. platform specific dependencies that don't even support having their dependency metadata extracted on other platforms)

In particular I am thinking of `pywin32` as it is now named (with a shim redirecting `pypiwin32`) which simply imports win32 apis in its `setup.py` -- if we generated and resolved against all platform ABI combos this would just throw some random `ImportError`, or bubble up as something else, on platforms other than windows. I don't know how often this occurs in terms of how many packages do this type of thing, but many users do require conditional installation of this library on windows.

Aye, having used all 3 clients, here some passing opinions of them:

#### pykafka
For a time pykafka was simply better than kafka-python.  It was the first to have a balanced consumer.  pykafka does not have good dynamic topic production support (#354), which makes it hard to use for some use cases.  Now that the Kafka API supports managing and balancing consumer groups itself, pykafka's interface feels a little fragmented.  The community around pykafka is excellent.  We have had several production issues, all of which have been pretty quickly responded to and fixed.

#### kafka-python
I actually like kafka-python's interface the best of all.  It feels the most clean.  However, even the newer version seems to perform fairly poorly for synchronous production (yes I know, never the answer bla bla), so I don't think we will use it in the future.  Plus, I like the idea of outsourcing the work of the client to librdkafka, and kafka-python does not have this.

#### confluent-kafka-python
So far so good.  It is very new, but I like using it and it seems to perform really well.  It'd be nice if the consumer interface returned an iterable.

I think Wikimedia will end up using confluent-kafka-python in the near future.  pykafka's lack of dynamic topic support is a no go for us, and kafka-python's performance isn't good enough.

Using librdkafka seems like the right thing to do, as it is so widely used in many different languages and environments that you can generally expect it to be very solid.  Wikimedia has an interest in a robust nodejs Kafka client as well, and I expect we will leverage librdkafka for that too.

I'm not familiar with USB devices in docker containers. python-adb doesn't need root if android's adb doesn't need it. If you have a common Android device, udev rules normally assign it to a group that your user has access to. If you don't, or you don't have the udev rules in the docker container, then you're going to need one of the following:
* Root, or
* Add a group your user is a part of to the device (either manually through sudo or automatically through udev), or
* Add your user to the group that the usb device is being assigned to

The way to find the usb device's user/group is to look at /dev/bus/usb/<bus>/<device>. You get <bus> and <device> by looking at `dmesg | tail` when plugging the device in. It'll say something like: `usb 1-3: new full-speed USB device number 20 using ...` so take the 1 from `1-3` and the 20 and you get /dev/bus/usb/001/020:

`ls -l /dev/bus/usb/001/020`

That should show you `root plugdev` or `root root`. If it says plugdev, ensure your user is in plugdev (most Linux flavors do that by default) with `groups | grep plugdev`. If it says root, then you need udev rules or you can manually do `sudo chgrp plugdev /dev/bus/usb/001/020` and you'll have access (until you unplug it or it reboots, then you'll have to do that again, with 021 since device numbers increment).

Copy-pasting @HereticSK comment here:

> If we are to mimic the cumulative return for the algorithm that holding N equal weighted sub-portfolios and rebalancing them every N period, I wonder if it would make more sense to compute the one-period return as np.nanmean((1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1)?
> 
> Compared to the original algorithm, it computes geometric mean before arithmetic mean. For a given period, the one-period return for the whole portfolio equals the average of one-period returns for N sub-portfolios. On the other hand, assuming each sub-portfolio grows at a steady rate within each rebalancing cycle, the one-period return for each sub-portfolio is given by the geometric mean of the corresponding N-period return, i.e. (1 + ret)**(1. / period) - 1). Combining these two steps, gives the one-period return of the whole portfolio equals np.nanmean((1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1).
> 
> Moreover, in the original algorithm, ((np.nanmean(ret) + 1) ** (1. / period)) - 1, the N-period returns for each sub-portfolio is averaged first. Somehow that seems like all these N-period returns are realized at this particular period, while they are actually realized at subsequent N periods. So I think it would make more sense to first compute (1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1), as an estimation of the realized return for a sub-portfolio on the given period, and then to average over all the sub-portfolios.

Okay, lots of stuff to respond to here.

> my "venv" folder was part of my skip_files config

I suspected as such, glad you figured it out.

> but it seem quite weird and inefficient to be loading the WHOLE venv directory rather than just the site-packages subfolder

This is one of many reasons we don't recommend using a virtualenv for vendoring. You can setup `skip_files` to only upload the site-packages folder.

> I think I'm going to just sit tight until someone points me to the valid official google instructions for local dev setup....

The instructions are [here](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/tools/using-libraries-python-27). The vendoring link is broken but it is supposed to link to [this heading](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/tools/using-libraries-python-27?hl=en#installing_a_library) in the same page.

In summary:
1. Add `vendor.add('lib')` to `appengine_config.py`.
2. Use `pip install -t lib -r requirements.txt`.

These are the steps used in all of our samples for App Engine standard that require third-party packages.

From what I understand, this is where things deviated for you:
1. You initially used a virtualenv and a symlinked lib folder, neither of which are documented or really recommended by us, for various reasons.
2. You had your virtualenv ignored in `app.yaml`.

From what I can gather, you chose this route because `pip install -t` failed for you. The error you pasted above seems to implicate [this known issue with homebrew Python](https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/Homebrew-and-Python.md#note-on-pip-install---user). They have a workaround in their documentation.

It seems my actions here to ameliorate this are:
1. Fix the vendor link in the docs.
2. Add a note about homebrew Python not working with `pip install -t` and link to their workaround.

I feel your pain here, I've been personally working on improving this situation. Please let me know if there's anything more I can do at this time to make this easier.

@philibuster Can you give some sample code using this or some description of why you'd want to be able to change the project on the fly.

This change is a little too ad-hoc for the object model, but that doesn't mean we can't take the spirit of the change and turn it into something useful.

----

A subscription has 4 properties

```python
name
topic
ack_deadline
push_endpoint
```

and you could accomplish essentially what you want with

```python
topic1 = Topic(name, project=project1)
topic2 = Topic(name, project=project2)

subscription1 = Subscription(sub_name, topic1, ack_deadline=AD, push_endpoint=PE)
subscription2 = Subscription(sub_name, topic2, ack_deadline=AD, push_endpoint=PE)
```

but it could be more convenient to have something which preserved all properties except for the topic via:

```python
subscription2 = Subscription.copy_from(subscription1, topic=topic2)
```

Even more, you want the same topic name, but just a different project, so this still isn't quite the same.

@albypeace thank you for sharing your idea! I was also thinking how to limit bandwidth on all ports so that all the users can have a speed limit and will not fight for the traffic between each other and will not take traffic from other server's services like SSH, etc.

Your idea of using traffic shaping tool `tc` is great, but it's not complete. In terms of `tc` there are two types of traffic:

- *egress* - outgoing traffic
- *ingress* - incoming traffic

The `tc` can do shaping only of *egress* traffic and for *ingress* traffic it has only *policy rules* that do not allow bandwidth control. Therefore, your rules of iptables with `-A INPUT` will not work in general case. They may work only if you use a patched kernel (and patched iptables) that allows it (for example, see project [IMQ](https://github.com/imq/linuximq/wiki)). [IFB](https://wiki.linuxfoundation.org/networking/ifb) is a successor of IMQ and looks good, but it's a more CPU consuming than creating a virtual network adapter - which is the solution that I suggest. In this case you can move the traffic between the network adapter `eth0` to the virtual `veth0`, and in such case the *ingress* traffic on `eth0` can be shaped as *egress* traffic on `veth0`.

Summarizing, in your current example bandwidth limiting works only for the server's outgoing traffic. To do traffic shaping of incoming traffic additional steps are required.

I'd like to add a few more thoughts - possibly from a different perspective. 

Executing things out of band is scary to me, because of the A/B stack dependencies and execution order that you mentioned above @ejholmes.  Nested stacks force this on you - for good reason..but there are drawbacks of course (that's why we're here!).  With the flexibility of stacker we would want to maintain robustness and consistency of stack deploys.  Today it's tough to get into a "half baked" situation with `stacker build`.   I tend to want to say "if stacker creates the changeset, stacker must execute the changeset", and a user shouldn't go to cloudformation to do it. 

The moment we create a changeset and leave it there,  someone could trip us up.  But stacker could control that more consistently.  I'm suggesting it should. 

The simple MVP I'd probably support the most are three new commands: (better names maybe!)

```
stacker create-changesets
stacker describe-changesets (accumulates all changesets in all stacks)
stacker execute-changesets
```

 _If this was in a pipeline you could set up policies to only allow your pipeline to execute the changesets. In effect solving the "out of band" risk.  (A more complicated option would be to involve cloudformation wait handles, so the changeset would already be executed and it would just be waiting for stacker to let it continue.. but that's going to be very involved)._ 

If your pipeline can execute `stacker create-changeset` it should be able to `stacker execute-changeset`.   I'm kind of missing the need for the lambda.  Your pipeline (I would think) could control notifications and just let the user go to cloudformation to view the changeset..  or a pipeline dashboard could make calls to `stacker describe-changeset` that would be handy on the CLI as well. (I'm really wanting  cli / pipeline parity). 

My motivations: 
* simplicity
* no state
* no polling etc

I feel like I'm missing the need for the lambda though.  Feel free to explain it here or ping me on the slack channel.  (might just be bswift there? )

My primary interest in stacker is for pipelines.   And I'm not sure I could "ease" an ops group into this without a separate create and execute step.  So I'm quite interested in helping out here. 

Again, just my perspective.  Others surely have different use cases.  Possibly the simplest solution wouldn't get everyone what they want, but could give everyone a little bit, and be enough to build on?

Yes - for libraries with native bits, its expected that the resulting pex will only run on (interpreters,platforms) it has built distributions for.  

The 1st issue is generally not a problem; ie: a pex built on 2.7.6 on a given platform (say OSX), will work when run under python 2.7.10.  Unfortunately you hit a problem described here where the two interpreters are not compatible at a lower level: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11878666/python-undefined-symbol-pyunicodeucs2-decodeutf8

You might be able to work around this particular issue if and only if you have uniform machine environments, when this is implemented: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/1768
At that point you can embed the full path to the specific interpreter the pex was built with since you know all machines will have the same (compatible) interpreter at that same path.

Looking at the second problem - pants allows you to build a pex that supports multiple platforms.
The docs touch on this here: https://pantsbuild.github.io/howto_develop.html#building-a-pants-pex-for-production

You can do this globally for all your pants built pexes in a repo with the pants.ini `[python-setup] platforms` list option (defaults globally to `['current']`, or in a python_binary directly like so:
```python
python_binary(
  ...
  platforms=[		
   'current',		
   'linux-x86_64',		
   'macosx-10.4-x86_64',		
  ],
...
)
```

That platforms configuration says always build for osx and linux.  This requires though that you have a bdist repo with eggs or wheels for each dependency you have 1-per platform.  So you'd need to build OSX wheels and place them there and also build a set of linux wheels.

This doesn't magically work unless all your linux targets have the same libs.  If you target, for example, both Cent OS 5 and 6, you'll hit issues at runtime with certain libs mismatching (If you built the platform specific wheels on Cent OS 6 they'll likelys fail on Cent OS 5).

@fhieber Here's the response I got from the folks in the know about the cuDNN details.

```
cudnn does not have a notion of padding, and it does not put padding anywhere. cudnn knows the length of each sequence in a batch, and operates only on valid entries. The input to cudnn is not a 3d padded tensor, but a list of 2d tensors with progressively smaller batch sizes (because shorter sequences drop out). Same for the output.

```
It's not a padded tensor, really, but more like a jagged tensor (analogous to a jagged matrix). In that case, if you want padding, then it will not be reversed, since there is either no padding (jagged tensor) or a padded tensor where padding is seen as valid entries.

Would it be possible at all to use jagged/unpadded tensors in MxNet and Sockeye somehow, or would that complicate things? If not, then SequenceReverse and non-cuDNN RNNs (RNNs at the graph level) may be the only way to deal with the current situation. It would be nice if padding could be completely removed, then cuDNN could handle "valid" (unpadded) sequence reversion.

For reference:

There are three elements that play a role:
* Each format specifies a "mode" that it can handle (single images, multiple images, volumes, or multiple volume). 
* Each format specifies extensions that it supports.
* Each format can report on a per-file basis whether it can read/write a format, and can inspect the first few bytes to make this decision.
* Formats are ordered to give preference (e.g. Pillow > PIL).

Imageio first selects all formats that conform to the mode and extension and then asks these formats whether they can handle the current request. The first format to return `True` will be used. If none of the selected formats handles the request (e.g. because the extension is not known), then all formats are again given a chance to handle the request.

This procedure is already complex enough as it is; thought its relatively predictable. The ordering (basically the order of imports in `plugins/__init__.py`) seems sufficient to assign priorities. A weighting will make things more flexible, but also less predictable.

_Cui bono?_ I am not particularly convinced that having the code base in the dev docs particularly helps anyone, unless it's via the deliberate link from another part of the documentation - in which case we can do the explicit import as we do in some places to bring them into the codebase.

Having actively onboarded many (N > 10) people into Kolibri, I have yet to see a single person look at the code on the dev docs (it is hard enough to get them to look at the docs at all). I think anything that reduces the irrelevant information is probably going to have a net benefit, and if it seems to be having a deleterious effect, we can always reverse this commit.

Taking React as an example, to get good performance, the same kinds of checks that we would want to do with a nested Marionette View implementation would still be applied if we were to use a Virtual DOM.

To improve performance using shouldComponentUpdate (a method that checks whether a change in state should result in a change in the DOM) is highly recommended - https://facebook.github.io/react/docs/component-specs.html#updating-shouldcomponentupdate

What this means is that if we have enough components to make a Marionette aggressive rendering engine slow, we would also have enough to make a virtual DOM slow, due to the large amount of virtual DOM diffing we would have to do, and we would still have to make the kinds of distinctions we would want in a highly nested Marionette implementation.

As such, I think your table presents a false dichotomy - I believe it should read thusly:

             | Aggressive re-rendering | View-managed re-rendering
----------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------
vDOM now |       High up-front dev time. Will give poor performance on anything but very simple apps.           |    Most complexity. A good use case for vDOM (or IDOM), since view-specific re-rendering is required to make vDOM performant.
vDOM later |               Creates large, unwieldy Views with poor separation of concerns. Will encounter performance issues unless app is very simple. Lowest up-front dev time, but sort of a gamble that performance will still be *good enough*, and will leave a messy refactor to divide up the monolithic templates into nested views. Very difficult to unit test precisely and well due to the sprawling area of concern.               |                    Reasonably high up-front dev time, but *probably* less than using a direct VDOM or IDOM implementation due to institutional familiarity with this nested approach in Backbone in KA Lite. This allows performance now with some complexity, but also allows for unit testing of modularized code, meaning that we can be more sure of the exact function of the view code. A transition to a VDOM or IDOM implementation could then be made seamlessly in the future, simply by modifying the way we compile handlebars templates to compile them into an IDOM or VDOM compatible format, and the render method of Marionette to cause them to be applied via an IDOM or VDOM engine.

*TL;DR* nested views are modular, testable, and improve performance whether we use VDOM/IDOM or not. They also do not impinge on our future capacity to implement a VDOM/IDOM engine, precisely because we can avoid doing direct DOM manipulation due to our heavily nested views.

SSE is not a separate animal. The client (web UI) connects to the backend (IPWB server) and starts listening on a channel. Each time the backend finds something worth telling the client, it simply emits an event data on that channel which can then be received by the client and acted upon (update pieces of information on the page). After the initial  connection, it is a one-way communication, that is why it is called a Server Sent Event.

The `quickrandom.masking_keys` generator that I linked to and gave benchmarks for is a 5 line function that does explicit batching. This adds a small amount of memory overhead for the buffer (400 bytes as implemented there, plus a bit more for the generator stack frame etc.), which is per client or per thread if you want to avoid locking. The batching only gives a speedup of ~2.5x, though... urandom is just not that slow. But, I mean, I'm just reporting what happens on my laptop, if you want to check for yourself you can always copy/paste my code and try it :-).

Also worth noting that the numbers there are with CPython 3.5, and there was a significant rework of Linux os.urandom in 3.5 (switching to using `getrandom`), so it's possible the numbers will look different on 2.7.

> how does it scale with multiple processes / threads calling into os.urandom?

Well, this is one of those questions where the only possible way to answer is to try running the workload you care about on the full system you care about and see what happens :-). In modern kernels random.c maintains multiple internal CPRNGs so that multiple CPUs can call into urandom simultaneously without contending, so in theory you "ought" to be fine. For whatever that's worth.

> because you've spent significant time on it, and I don't have time right now to continue the discussion (I am not convinced) - but I don't want to leave an impression I'd be brushing this off

Don't worry about me :-). It's your project and you should obviously make whatever decision you feel is best for your users. I started looking into this because I was poking at [wsproto](https://github.com/jeamland/wsproto) and trying to figure out what the "right thing" even was; if nothing else this conversation has definitely helped clarify for me what the actual trade-offs are.

Like I said, I personally would not choose to violate the RFC and potentially put some small but unknown number of users at risk in order to get a small and potentially irrelevant speedup. I have no idea how many broken intercepting proxies are out there on the network, or even if any exist. The big browser vendors seem to think it's a risk worth worrying about, and they probably have as much data as is possible for anyone to get (even though I'm sure they wish they had more too!), so I'm inclined to follow their lead. But, I mean, you're the one who users will complain to if things are too slow, and you're the one who'll have to explain to them why you decided to go out of your way to violate the RFC if they do get pwned :-). And either way, at this point I think the thread has mostly served its purpose of making clear what the actual risks/trade-offs are (at least as well as we can know them), so now it's just a judgment call.

Sticky sessions don't inherently break 12-factor ideals...but they have historically had a tendency to do so by enabling application state to be stored in the running instance. To be absolutely clear here, my goal is not to store application state but to ensure app instances can be made aware of http connection state as appropriate.

The WebSockets upgrade handshake, for example, has a [standard mechanism] to signal initiation of this connection-level selective-stickyness such that the connection to the application instance is kept alive though a proxy that would [normally terminate] it. Should the addition of WebSockets preclude the possibility of a 12-factor app?

I contest the statement that
> Sticky sessions are a violation of twelve-factor and should never be used or relied upon. Session state data is a good candidate for a datastore that offers time-expiration, such as Memcached or Redis. <cite>[12factor.net]</cite>

...if the application implementation is stateless from a _user_  or _auth_ session perspective. Storing protocol state in memcached/redis results in a game of race condition whackamole (at least for me). What I'm looking for here are supporting pragmatic equivalents to the [keepalive] directive in an HTTP/1.1 world.

> Clients are also encouraged to consider the use of Connection:
> keep-alive in requests carefully; while they can enable persistent
> connections with HTTP/1.0 servers, clients using them will need to
> monitor the connection for "hung" requests (which indicate that the
> client ought stop sending the header field), and this mechanism ought
> not be used by clients at all when a proxy is being used.
> <cite>[RFC 7230 A.1.2.]</cite>

For a web application needing a full duplex stream to a remote server, be that via WebSockets, socket.io, engine.io, sockjs, browserchannel, webchannel, or something hand-rolled, knowing when that HTTP/1.1 keepalive-by-default connection has "hung" is critical.

This is an area where many existing PaaS providers are lagging. Would be really cool to see Deis competitve with Modulus, Nodejitsu, and maybe even MDG's Galaxy!

[standard mechanism]: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455#section-1.8
[normally terminate]: http://nginx.com/blog/http-keepalives-and-web-performance/
[12factor.net]: http://12factor.net/processes#vi_processes
[keepalive]: http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_upstream_module.html#keepalive
[RFC 7230 A.1.2.]: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#appendix-A.1.2
[RFC 7230 p.53]: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#page-53

Sorry, I do think that there is a problem. Yes, I do use numpy

This looks like a timing issue between the packets producer (the network, using a wide filter) and the consumer (_pcap.next()_ called from Python). Memory consumption grows because packets get buffered faster than removed from the buffer.

In order to verify this hypothesis, you can add a time.sleep in the loop and we must see a higher inclined line (given the same number of network packets captured).

My suggestions are the following:

- Use a narrow filter
- Use a dispatch callback instead of manually calling next (better performance)
- Set the Pcap buffer size in order to drop packets (still not possible in Pcapy, see #17)

FWIW pysamstats (https://github.com/alimanfoo/pysamstats) has a pad option
which will cause data to be emitted even for non-covered regions. To use
from the command line:

pysamstats --type coverage --pad example.bam > example.coverage.txt

To use from python and obtain a numpy array:

import pysam
import pysam
mybam = pysam.AlignmentFile('/path/to/your/bamfile.bam')
a = pysamstats.load_coverage(mybam, chrom='Pf3D7_01_v3', pad=True)

pysamstats is implemented in cython so should be as quick as samtools.

Upon further investigation, VarEvents doesn't like that value changing types.

I do have concerns about nodes that will return integers as their value. There could possible be a node that returns positive and negative values which will make this logic equally ambiguous.

You could set the value, when unknown, to:
```python
-1 * float("inf")
```
 
The other interesting thing about changing this logic is that Home Assistant will recognize unknown values as "ON" rather than "OFF". This completely changes the behavior in Home Assistant. I'm not sure how I feel about that. Do you plan on updating the code in Home Assistant to support this change?

My general feedback on some of the other creates (the three or four super long ones):

- It seems like there are things which are or should be enums and thus exposed as choice lists (for example, os-family).
- in general, where there is a parameter called `--{foo}-id`, if that ID is an ARM resource ID, then that parameter should just be called `{foo}` and it should accept a name or ID.
- you might be able to shorten some of the options since you have them in argument groups. For example: the Job Manager Task arguments all start with --job-manager-task. If you can remove that and not have any naming conflicts, it might be worth considering.

[`@ccordoba12` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3753073): I've been following the notebook development quite closely and I think @ellisonbg is doing a terrific job! I just have a couple of thoughts to add to this discussion:
1. My original request was not directed exactly towards collapse/expand but more broadly to cell grouping. Collapse/expand is one of the things you can do with grouping and it's very important. I think it's more or less the equivalent of the "include document" functionality in Lyx: it lets you hide some parts of the document to focus your attention on others.
2. However, there are two more things you can do with grouping (as far as I can tell):
   - Delete/move a group. In Mathematica this is as easy as: select the group bracket (the blue vertical line, I mean), hit _Ctrl+X_ and then _Ctrl+V_ in another part of the notebook. I read in the last @fperez blog post that you plan to introduce an outline explorer similar to the Lyx one. Since this one also has the ability to move sections, I hope you could reproduce this functionality in IPython (although that seems a bit hard to do).
   - Evaluate a particular group. Perhaps this is not well known but, again in Mathematica, if you select a group bracket and hit Shift+Enter, you can evaluate just that group. I find this quite neat and useful but I don't know if you could implement it with current flat model.

As a side note: @ellisonbg, I haven't experienced in Mathematica the indent/dedent problem you described: to indent you just have to introduce a new heading level with a lower precedence from the previous one and Mathematica will create the group automatically until the next higher heading it finds. To dedent you change the type of your group heading to a higher precedence one.

To conclude: I'm not saying that Mathematica's way of doing things is the only possible one but that IMHO the cases I described are worth considering when you're taking the next design decisions about grouping.

Just my two cents,
Carlos

Ah I see. But maybe we could compute it just once as `1./np.sqrt(np.pi)` once at the module level instead of hardcoding the number.

Supporting real in-place operations is fundamentally hard with reverse-mode autodiff because, as David recently explained to me, you often need most of the intermediate forward values to evaluate the gradients on the backward pass (and you can't just regenerate them in the backward pass, at least not in general, though Dougal and David have a [paper on reversible learning](http://arxiv.org/abs/1502.03492)). The best we could hope for is to allow in-place syntax but collect forward values behind the scenes, discarding ones we don't need to look up on the backward pass.

Yes. It's described on pages 13-14 of http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.416.9240&rep=rep1&type=pdf. The R-operator evaluates the Jacobian-vector product and is equivalent to forward-mode autodiff.
While the Hessian-vector product Hv for a function f(theta) has a concise implementation without the R-op (it's `grad(dot(v, grad(f)))`, although that takes twice as much computation as `grad(Rop(f, v))`), the (generalized Gauss Newton)-vector product Gv for a function f(g(theta)) needs the R-op/jvp (it's `grad(dot(theta, grad(dot(Rop(g, v), grad(f)))))` or `grad(dot(theta, Rop(f, Rop(g, v))))`.)

```python
class BaseScheduler:
    """ Simplest scheduler; activates agents one at a time, in the order
    they were added.

    Assumes that each agent added has a *step* method which takes no arguments.

    (This is explicitly meant to replicate the scheduler in MASON).

    model = None
    steps = 0
    time = 0
    agents = []

    def __init__(self, model):
        """ Create a new, empty BaseScheduler. """
        self.model = model
        self.steps = 0
        self.time = 0
        self.agents = []
```

Anyway, here's the time I get from that file with current dateutil:

```sh

2016-02-18 05:00:00

real    0m4.808s
user    0m4.727s
sys     0m0.063s
```

...and with this patch applied:

```sh
$ time python rruleset.py 
2016-02-18 05:00:00

real    0m0.576s
user    0m0.486s
sys     0m0.071s
```

The more rrules you have, the more valuable this patch becomes. Hope that helps! :)

The scanning methods, such as `_walk_relative_paths` already take several parameters, and I'm reluctant to add another one for excluding directories.  How about defining a `FileFilter` interface, and  replacing the `exclusions` and `inclusions` with a `FileFilter`?  It could handle filtering both files and directories:

```
@six.add_metaclass(ABCMeta)
class FileFilter(object):
    """
    Policy object that decides which files should be included in a scan.
    """

    @abstractmethod
    def should_exclude_file(self, file_path):
        """
        Given the full path of a file, should it be excluded from the scan?

        :param file_path: The path of the file, relative to the root directory
                          being scanned.  This is a file, not a directory, so
                          the path will never end in '/'.
        :return: True iff excluded.
        """

    @abstractmethod
    def should_exclude_directory(self, dir_path):
        """
        Given the full path of a directory, should all of the files in it be
        excluded from the scan?

        :param dir_path: The path of the directory, relative to the root directory
                         being scanned.  This is a directory, so the path will
                         always end in '/'.
        :return: True iff excluded .
        """
```

Here's the solution for pvsyst from Matlab, and python code to read the csv file into an OrderedDict.

[PVsyst_demo_model.txt](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/files/425749/PVsyst_demo_model.txt)


import numpy as np
from collections import OrderedDict


specs = {key: None for key in ['ns', 'aisc','bvoc','type']}
paramlist  = ['IL_ref', 'Io_ref', 'eG', 'Rsh_ref', 'Rsh0', 'Rshexp', 'Rs_ref', 'gamma_ref', 'mugamma']
varlist = ['Iph', 'Io', 'Rsh', 'Rs', 'u']
pvsyst = OrderedDict(key = paramlist + varlist)

with open('PVsyst_demo_model.txt', 'r') as f:

    Ns, aIsc, bVoc, descr = f.readline().split(',')
    
    specs['ns'] = int(float(Ns))
    specs['aisc'] = float(aIsc)
    specs['bvoc'] = float(bVoc)
    specs['type'] = descr
    
    IL_ref, Io_ref, eG, Rsh_ref, Rsh0, Rshexp, Rs_ref, gamma_ref, mugamma = f.readline().split(',')
    for key in paramlist:
        pvsyst[key] = float(eval(key))
    
    strN = f.readline()
    N = int(strN)

    Iph = np.empty(N)
    Io = np.empty_like(Iph)
    Rsh = np.empty_like(Iph)
    Rs = np.empty_like(Iph)
    u = np.empty_like(Iph)
    
    for k in range(0,N):
        Iph[k], Io[k], Rsh[k], Rs[k], u[k] = f.readline().split(',')

for var in varlist:
    pvsyst[var] = eval(var)

X[..., 1] = |  0  |  0  |  1  |  0  |  0  | ... |  0  |  1  | ... |  0  |  0  |


Therefore, the output Y for the sequence above is equivalent to:

Y[...] = 0.5 * 0 + 0.7 * 0 + 0.3 * 1 + ... + 0.2 * 0


Which should explain the line from the code:

y[...] = np.sum(X[..., 0] * X[..., 1])

> If it would have been possible (but I don't think it currently is in Lasagne), you should also save the internal state of the optimizer so you can restore that as well when you continue the training

Yes, this is indeed important. It's easily possible by saving the values of all keys of the update dictionary:
```python
# for saving:
values1 = [p.get_value() for p in updates.keys()]
values2 = lasagne.layers.get_all_param_values(network, trainable=False)
# for restoring:
for p, value in zip(updates.keys(), values1):
    p.set_value(value)
lasagne.layers.set_all_param_values(network, values2, trainable=False)
```
Note that the update dictionary does not include non-trainable weights (e.g., batch normalization running averages), so they need to be stored and restored separately.
