其中 `ansitowin32.py` 文件中定义了如下内容:

```python
class StreamWrapper(object):
    '''
    Wraps a stream (such as stdout), acting as a transparent proxy for all
    attribute access apart from method 'write()', which is delegated to our
    Converter instance.
    '''
    def __init__(self, wrapped, converter):
        # double-underscore everything to prevent clashes with names of
        # attributes on the wrapped stream object.
        self.__wrapped = wrapped
        self.__convertor = converter

    def __getattr__(self, name):
        return getattr(self.__wrapped, name)

    def write(self, text):
        self.__convertor.write(text)

    def fileno():
        raise OSError()
```

> @alexanderrichards The problem here seems to be that if the lock cannot be acquired then the code within the with block should not be executed. There is no way to do this with a context manager, see http://stackoverflow.com/a/12594323/96704.

yup your quite right, momentary lapse on my part. As I recall it was stated in the refused PEP on this "The with shouldn't impersonate an if statement". However I see no reason why we cant try the lock first and then use a context to remember to release it.
```python
from contextlib import contextmanager

def release(rlock):
    yield rlock
    rlock.release()

l = RLock()
if l.acquire(blocking=False):
    with release(l):
        <blah>
```

note that a new context is needed as `RLock` defines `__enter__` already which will try to acquire the lock and at this point we only care about releasing it. Either this way or create a custom `RLock` with a nullop `__enter__` but I think this is clearer.

Both auto-white balance and auto-exposure controls are supported in librealsense SDK 2.0 via the same API that was available in SDK v1.0 : `rs2_get_option(...)` and `rs2_set_option(...)`.

Note that the white-balance is RGB-only control, and applies exclusively to D415, D435 and SR300.

The default values of those controls are preset in the camera's firmware.
You can run `rs-enumerate-devices` utility to read those default for the cameras you have.
For instance, D435 with FW 5.9.11 ( [currently recommended](https://downloadcenter.intel.com/download/27792/Latest-Firmware-for-Intel-RealSense-D400-Product-Family?v=t)):

Hey @moonshoes87 I don't think it's a matter reading in data that is the problem here when it reads in it just tosses the intensity data to the side so it doesn't fall apart same as it's always worked. It's the writing out when the analysis is done and this is a problem I've struggled with for a while.

I had originally assumed that the idea with Magic 3 was to have a simple yet extensive set of vocab so you would only need one line for a single magnetic component in the specimens table and one measurement record per actual physical measurement in the measurement table; so I built Demag GUI with this in mind. So it does collapse the rows trying to merge them in a sensible manner to simplify the table. These functions that Luke put together are ipmag implementations of these two command line programs:

- revtest.py (this test is an implementation of the Tauxe bootstrap reversal test that can now be done with ipmag.bootstrap_reversal_test)
- revtest_MM1990.py (this test is an implementation of the Watson V common-mean/reversal test that also reports the critical angle as formulated by MM1990that can now be done with ipmag.MM1990_reversal_test)

MM1990 is the Watson V test with the additional formulation of the critical angle which provides a way to obtain a measure of the amount of information available for the test. *Note that one can simply do the Watson's V test using ipmag.watson_common_mean which is the function underlying ipmag.MM1990_reversal_test*. The issue is that if there are only a few observations, or the directions have quite low precision, then there are situations where the null hypothesis of a common mean cannot be rejected using Watson's V test (thereby constituting a "positive" reversal test), but where that result isn't meaningful or robust. 

Consider two populations of directions with quite low precision that have slightly different means, but are far from antipodal from one another:

```
directions1 = ipmag.fishrot(k=1,n=4,Dec=20,Inc=20)
```
```
directions2 = ipmag.fishrot(k=1,n=4,Dec=340,Inc=-20)
```

These means are far from antipodal from one another, but if you draw these populations, take the antipode of one of them and conduct a Watson V test you will get the result that the null hypothesis of a common mean can not be rejected. The MM1990 formulation recasts the V statistic as a critical angle and in this test would argue that the reversal test is indeterminate (not enough information to draw a robust conclusion). This classification makes sense because the data are so bad and the result that they share are common mean is not particularly useful which why it is helpful to have a classification that says the test is indeterminate. These data and there actual (far from antipodal) means as specified in fishrot are plotted below:

That's not actually true (anymore) in current master since f2ecd8927e023b8b083d0f1cba525f61c4691ecf: `reference_internal` is now implemented (and documented) as a shortcut for specifying both `reference` and `keep_alive<0, 1>()`.  So this might actually have implications for backwards compatibility if people are using reference_internal with nullptr-returnable functions.

Boost.Python also [appears to support this](http://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_61_0/libs/python/doc/html/reference/function_invocation_and_creation/models_of_callpolicies.html#function_invocation_and_creation.models_of_callpolicies.boost_python_with_custodian_and_) (presumably for the same reasons @GlenWalker has given):

  > If the custodian object does not support weak references *and is not `None`*, an appropriate exception will be thrown.

and so without this, we aren't *quite* equivalent to Boost.Python's `with_custodian_and_ward` and `..._postcall` as claimed in the "Additional call policies" documentation.

On that note, assuming that @wjakob decides to accept the change, it deserves an addition to `docs/advanced.rst` that documents the feature in the Additional call policies section: while I see the value after reading your posts, I don't find it *immediately* obvious whether it should work as you expect or in the current fashion, which is just the sort of thing that deserves a documentation sentence or two.

There are tradeoffs: the biggest issue/objection was that it would be encoding the access mechanism by using a fundamentally different C++ type for safe versus unsafe access arrays, despite the type representing exactly the same underlying object.  You'd need yet another C++ type (either under a separate name, or via a template parameter--either way still a distinct type) to distinguish between read-only and mutable input arrays, so now we'd have 3 C++ types to represent exactly the same object--and they'd need implicit conversions defined so that you could switch between them at will (with mutability checks when switching from a read-only to mutable type).  There would also be a large amount of method duplication to support it all.

So it's possible, but I'm not really convinced that it's worth the cost just to avoid needing to write `auto r = a.unchecked<2>()`.

The main problem here occurs if Certbot's Nginx plugin has to start Nginx and you later try to stop/start Nginx with `systemctl`. On most modern Linux systems, `service` is a wrapper around `systemctl`. If Nginx was already running and Certbot just has to reload Nginx to make it pick up changes to its configuration, there's not a problem. (This is why commands like `sudo certbot --authenticator standalone --installer nginx --pre-hook "service nginx stop" --post-hook "service nginx start"` work and `certbot renew` does not. The `renew` subcommand runs post-hooks right before Certbot exits while the `run` subcommand (which is the default) runs it directly after obtaining the cert before the installer is run.)

If Certbot's Nginx plugin has to start Nginx, it does so by using the `nginx` command directly rather than going through `systemctl` or `service`. In the couple systems I looked at where `service` doesn't invoke `systemctl`, everything should still work fine. The problem is that `systemctl stop <service>` often doesn't work if that service wasn't started by systemd. (On Ubuntu 16.04, this is because Nginx is stopped using the value `ExecStop` which isn't run if the service wasn't started with `ExecStart` as described [here](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd.service.html). On CentOS 7, systemd keeps track of Nginx's PID and sends a signal to it, however, if Nginx wasn't started with systemd, it doesn't know the PID).

I don't immediately have a solution in mind, but that is my understanding of the problem here.

> Actually, we can use the transferred_amount to order the messages (it's also a monotonically increasing value), the nonce can be moved out of the envelope into a transport detail of Raiden.
> 
> NVM, transferred_amount is monotonically increasing but it does not change with every message.

Right now - Raiden implements two unidirectional channels (Alice -> Bob, and Bob -> Alice). So all payments are one-way. To me, this would allow the removal of the nonce as transferred_amount can only increase monotonically (i.e. largest transferred_amount represents latest payment). 

I was thinking about it last night and I'm not sure why Raiden is implementing two unidirectional channels. In Bitcoin it makes sense - if we want to naively implement bidirectional payment channels then each payment needs to decrement nLockTime (i.e. the Absolute Lock Time). If we have two unidirectional channels, then we can have nearly infinite small payments until we run out of bitcoins. 

However, in Ethereum, the **nonce** can be incremented for every new payment which isn't possible in Bitcoin. This would allow Raiden to implement bidirectional payments in a straightforward way. 
Everytime I send a payment - I sign the new final balance of both parties. If the counterparty isn't happy with that payment - they broadcast the previously agreed payment. This works as any payment sent to the Blockchain will require both of our signatures (so we both agree the final payment). 

In the worst case: there should only be two transactions broadcast to the Blockchain (counterpartys claim of the latest payment; and if the counterparty lied, then I broadcast my latest claim of the payment). 

The benefit of the above approach over unidirectional channels is that it never needs to close. We are essentially splitting the deposit in each new payment; and the balance can go back/forth. 

Of course - if the above was to be implemented - it would take some time - so it might be best sticking with the unidirectional approach *for now* and then moving to it sometime in the future.

```
from tpot import TPOTClassifier
from tpot.config import classifier_config_dict
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import numpy as np
# add 2 operators into the classifier_config_dict
classifier_config_dict['tpot.builtins.CategoricalSelector'] = {
    'threshold': [10],
    'minimum_fraction': [0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25],
    'sparse': [False]
}

classifier_config_dict['tpot.builtins.ContinuousSelector'] = {
    'threshold': [10],
    'svd_solver': ['randomized'],
    'iterated_power': range(1, 11)
}

# load data
iris = load_iris()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(iris.data.astype(np.float64),
    iris.target.astype(np.float64), train_size=0.75, test_size=0.25)

tpot = TPOTClassifier(generations=5, population_size=50, verbosity=2, config_dict=classifier_config_dict)
tpot.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(tpot.score(X_test, y_test))
```

@flodolo thanks for the reply!

>  Is there anything in particular that makes you think it didn't reach minimum functionality?

The first thing is the lack of docs - undocumented features don't exist, in my book. As a user, you have no idea what it is supposed to do, what is stable and what is going to change etc. For Python in particular this is vital - you have no compiler that will tell you the signature of a function changed. The only contract you have is docs.

Second, the fluent project home page lists JS, Python, and Rust implementations. However, the JS implementation contains an actual interpreter and a 'format' method - https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent.js/blob/master/fluent/src/context.js#L163 and so does the Rust implementation - https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent-rs/blob/master/src/context.rs#L90 . The Python implementation does not seem to have anything like that, and that's what I thought would be minimum functionality - because without it, you cannot actually generate translations, which is the **entire point** (from the perspective of anyone reaching the project via the fluent home page, like me).

Thanks for letting me know about it's use by Pontoon etc. This confirms what I had thought - I would consider this project to currently be an internal library used by Mozilla, which happens to have its own github page, but it's really just an internal library. The features it has are determined by Mozilla's current needs only.

**None of this is meant as a criticism** or some kind of demand to do more, in terms of code or docs. It's just that given the way it is advertised, it would be helpful to be clear about these things, especially that this is not really an implementation in the sense that is used for the JS/Rust projects, because you can't actually use it to generate translations. And even the parser - without docs, including some information about API stability plus a usable change log - is not the kind of thing you can build on.

Two lines in the README would suffice to fix this e.g.:

> This project contains only a parser for ftl files, which is being used by some Mozilla projects. It does not contain code to actually generate translations.

Plus, something saying whether such code is being worked on, or would be accepted as a patch, and what it should look like etc. For someone outside Mozilla like me, there is currently nothing about this project which suggests it is really "open for business" as an open source project.

]

> Limit the number of returned items by default.

What is the advantage of this? If a user queries a large area over a long time this will return the first page of results (if the default is 100), but the query will still be executed on the DHuS. As long as the DHuS doesn't split up long queries into multiple smaller queries that produce about 100 results this will not considerably reduce server load. It is possible that such behaviour might be implemented in the future but the current DHuS release does not contain anything alike in its code (I might be mistaken though, my Java game is pretty weak).

I do support having a reasonable default, that limits the amount a new user queries. In my opinion we already have such a default in place with the default time queried being NOW-1DAY to NOW. If a user extends that timeframe he deliberately wants more results and I suspect most if not all will extend the limit to a large number then anyway.

> Add a way to get the number of products matching a query on the server via the API.

I'm with @j08lue on this. If it is as easy as `len(products)` I don't see an advantage of including it as functionality in the API.

We used to do exactly what you are proposing, but the change was made in ff6d5ea4 to fix a bug in the case of non-ASCII characters in the path.

I have no idea what solution will solve both of these problems, but if you have one to propose, I'm all ears :-)

``FormElement.method`` and ``FormElement.action`` are not really necessary, since the ``ElementAPI`` has a ``__getitem__`` method for tag attributes. We provide shortcuts for frequently accessed properties (like ``value`` and ``text``).
